title: MCP Stack Registry - 8 Pre-Built Stacks
description: Curated MCP server combinations optimized for specific use cases. Each stack contains 4 servers selected for maximum synergy and minimal conflict.

stackCuratorAlgorithm.name: MCP Stack Curator v2
stackCuratorAlgorithm.description: Algorithmic approach to optimal 4-server MCP stack composition

principles.name: Complementarity
principles.description: Each server must provide a capability the other 3 cannot. No two servers should solve the same problem.

principles.name: Data Flow
principles.description: Servers must form a clear data pipeline: extract → process → store → verify. At least one server must produce output another consumes.

principles.name: Universal Base
principles.description: Memory MCP and Filesystem MCP are universal enhancers — they can join any stack without conflict. Only include when they actively improve the data flow.

principles.name: Conflict Avoidance
principles.description: Never combine Postgres + Supabase, two web search servers, or two file-writers on the same directory. These create race conditions and data conflicts.

principles.name: Minimum Surprise
principles.description: All 4 servers must be free, publicly available, and have active maintenance (commits within 6 months). No private or paywalled servers in pre-built stacks.

conflictMatrix.Postgres + Supabase: CRITICAL - Both modify database schema, creates migration conflicts
conflictMatrix.Brave Search + Serper: WARNING - Two web search servers produce redundant results
conflictMatrix.Filesystem + Filesystem: CRITICAL - Two file writers on same paths creates race conditions
conflictMatrix.GitHub + GitLab: CONDITIONAL - Only compatible if managing repos on separate platforms
conflictMatrix.Postgres + SQLite: COMPATIBLE - Postgres for production, SQLite for local analytics is fine
conflictMatrix.Memory + any server: COMPATIBLE - Memory MCP is a universal complement

synergyMatrix.Memory + any: Universal enhancer — stores preferences, context, and learned patterns
synergyMatrix.Sequential Thinking + any complex task: Structured reasoning before action reduces errors 40%
synergyMatrix.Filesystem + any artifact producer: Saves generated content to organized project structure
synergyMatrix.Brave Search + Fetch: Search finds sources, Fetch extracts full content — classic research pipeline
synergyMatrix.PictoFlux AI + Filesystem: Generate images, save to project assets — visual content pipeline
synergyMatrix.GitHub + Filesystem: Code changes tracked in git, files managed locally — development workflow
synergyMatrix.Brave Search + Sequential Thinking: Research findings structured into logical arguments
synergyMatrix.Postgres + Filesystem: Database exports to local files for backup and analysis

stacks.name: Creative Studio
stacks.tagline: From idea to visual - image generation meets design systems
synergy: PictoFlux generates images -> Filesystem saves them to project
Figma provides design context -> PictoFlux generates matching visuals
Memory stores brand preferences -> PictoFlux applies consistent styles
stacks.curatorNotes: These four servers cover the full creative pipeline: Figma supplies design specs, PictoFlux generates visuals from those specs, Filesystem persists assets, and Memory retains brand/style decisions across sessions.
stacks.dataFlow: Design specs (Figma) → Generate visuals (PictoFlux) → Save assets (Filesystem) → Recall brand rules (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses design file access but gains trend research for inspiration-first workflows
Loses image generation but gains structured creative brief planning before manual design

stacks.name: Research Engine
stacks.tagline: Deep research across web, academic, and code sources
synergy: Brave Search finds sources -> Fetch extracts full content
Arxiv provides papers -> Memory indexes findings
stacks.curatorNotes: Search (Brave) and extraction (Fetch) form the classic research pipeline, Arxiv adds academic depth that web search cannot reach, and Memory ensures no finding is lost across sessions.
stacks.dataFlow: Search sources (Brave) → Extract content (Fetch) → Academic depth (Arxiv) → Index findings (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses academic paper access but gains ability to generate visual summaries of research
Loses live web scraping but gains ability to save and organize research locally as files

stacks.name: Full-Stack Dev
stacks.tagline: Code, database, deploy - the complete development pipeline
synergy: GitHub manages code -> Filesystem edits files -> Postgres manages data
Sequential Thinking plans architecture -> Filesystem creates structure
mismatches: Avoid Postgres MCP and Supabase MCP simultaneously
stacks.curatorNotes: GitHub handles version control, Postgres manages production data, Filesystem edits project files locally, and Sequential Thinking ensures architectural decisions are reasoned through before code is written.
stacks.dataFlow: Plan architecture (Sequential Thinking) → Edit code (Filesystem) → Version control (GitHub) → Manage data (Postgres)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses production-grade database but gains lighter local analytics with no server setup
Loves repo management but gains ability to search for solutions and documentation during development

stacks.name: Content Machine
stacks.tagline: Write, optimize, publish - end-to-end content pipeline
synergy: Brave Search researches -> Memory stores brand voice -> Content written consistently
PictoFlux generates visuals -> Filesystem saves with content
stacks.curatorNotes: Brave Search researches topics, PictoFlux creates featured images, Filesystem organizes published content, and Memory preserves brand voice so every piece sounds consistent.
stacks.dataFlow: Research topics (Brave Search) → Generate visuals (PictoFlux) → Store content (Filesystem) → Recall brand voice (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses image generation but gains deep content extraction from competitor articles for research-heavy workflows
Loses web research but gains structured content outlining for long-form writing

stacks.name: Data Pipeline
stacks.tagline: Extract, transform, visualize - data engineering in a box
synergy: Fetch extracts -> Filesystem stages -> Postgres warehouses -> SQLite explores locally
mismatches: Do NOT use Postgres MCP and Supabase MCP together
stacks.curatorNotes: Fetch extracts raw data from the web, Filesystem stages intermediate ETL files, Postgres serves as the production warehouse, and SQLite provides lightweight local analytics — a complete data pipeline with no overlapping roles.
stacks.dataFlow: Extract data (Fetch) → Stage files (Filesystem) → Warehouse (Postgres) → Analyze locally (SQLite)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses local SQL analytics but gains persistent memory of pipeline configurations and schema decisions
Loses direct data scraping but gains broader discovery of data sources and APIs

stacks.name: Security Audit
stacks.tagline: Scan, analyze, report - comprehensive security review
synergy: GitHub scans remote -> Filesystem scans local -> comprehensive coverage
Brave Search finds CVEs -> Sequential Thinking assesses exploitability
stacks.curatorNotes: GitHub audits remote code, Filesystem scans local code, Brave Search looks up known CVEs, and Sequential Thinking systematically models threat vectors — full coverage from code to context to reasoning.
stacks.dataFlow: Scan remote code (GitHub) → Scan local code (Filesystem) → Lookup CVEs (Brave Search) → Model threats (Sequential Thinking)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses broad CVE search but gains ability to pull detailed vulnerability reports from specific advisory pages
Loses structured threat modeling but gains persistent tracking of past audit findings and remediation decisions

stacks.name: Agent OS
stacks.tagline: Memory, orchestration, reasoning - the autonomous agent foundation
synergy: The four pillars: Remember (Memory) -> Reason (Thinking) -> Act (Filesystem) -> Verify (Search)
stacks.curatorNotes: This is the canonical agent architecture: Memory provides persistent knowledge, Sequential Thinking enables multi-step reasoning, Filesystem allows actions on the world, and Brave Search grounds the agent in real-time information.
stacks.dataFlow: Recall context (Memory) → Reason about task (Sequential Thinking) → Execute actions (Filesystem) → Verify with reality (Brave Search)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses broad search discovery but gains deep content extraction for agents that need to read full pages
Loses general file I/O but gains code-specific operations for agents focused on software development tasks

stacks.name: Product Launch
stacks.tagline: Research, build, market, sell - from zero to launched
stacks.curatorNotes: Brave Search validates the market, GitHub ships the code, PictoFlux creates launch visuals, and Memory stores the launch playbook so nothing is forgotten across the chaotic launch period.
stacks.dataFlow: Research market (Brave Search) → Build product (GitHub) → Create assets (PictoFlux) → Store playbook (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses image generation but gains organized file management for launch documents, copy, and configs
Loses persistent launch playbook but gains structured decision-making for go/no-go launch criteria

alwaysCombine: Filesystem MCP + any server that produces artifacts
neverCombine: Postgres MCP + Supabase MCP (database access conflict)
Multiple file-write servers on same directory
conditionalCombine: GitHub MCP + GitLab MCP (only if managing repos on both platforms)
Postgres MCP + SQLite MCP (fine if Postgres is production and SQLite is local)
